planting.note: Start seeds indoors 8–10 weeks before last frost and transplant after all danger of frost passes in very well-drained, lean, sandy soil in full sun; rock purslane is perennial in frost-free zones (9–11) and treated as a tender annual in colder climates. Formerly classified as Calandrinia grandiflora. Like other succulent-leaved purslane relatives, it requires excellent drainage and thrives on neglect.

directions: Plant in very well-drained, lean, sandy to rocky soil in full sun; rock purslane is a Chilean coastal hill native producing large rosettes of thick, fleshy, gray-green succulent leaves of impressive scale (up to 6–8 inches long) and from spring through summer, tall, branching stems bearing multiple clusters of vivid, intense, hot-magenta-fuchsia flowers (1.5–2 inches across) in clear, pure, un-muddied color — one of the most visually arresting single colors available from any succulent or drought-tolerant plant. The contrast of the cool, gray-green, fleshy foliage with the hot, electric fuchsia-magenta flowers is striking and tropical in quality. In zones 9–11 it grows as a long-lived, semi-woody perennial up to 4 feet tall that blooms for much of the year in mild seasons. In colder zones it is grown as a tender annual from early-started seed. Formerly classified as Calandrinia grandiflora, it was reclassified into Cistanthe based on molecular evidence. Outstanding in containers, on dry rock slopes, and in the front of drought-tolerant borders in subtropical and Mediterranean-climate gardens.

planting.note: Start seeds indoors 8–10 weeks before last frost and transplant to sharply drained, lean soil in full sun after danger of frost passes; calandrinia is short-lived but self-sows in mild climates. In zones 7–9 it may overwinter in a sheltered position with excellent drainage. The flowers close in overcast conditions and open only in full sun.

directions: Plant in sharply drained, lean to moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; umbellate calandrinia is a compact Chilean native producing low mounds of narrow, slightly fleshy, slightly hairy leaves and in summer a succession of intense, brilliant crimson-magenta, five-petaled flowers (3/4 to 1 inch across) in loose, umbellate clusters on short stems — the flowers opening only in full sun and closing in shade or overcast conditions, creating a display that varies dramatically between bright sun and cloud cover. The saturation and purity of the crimson-magenta color is exceptional — a true, vivid, unmuddied crimson-red rather than pink or orange-red. It thrives in the lean, dry, rocky, and well-drained conditions of alpine and coastal rocky habitats in Chile. Outstanding in rock gardens, gravel gardens, and any lean, sun-baked position where its compact size and vivid color can be appreciated at close range. Self-sows in mild climates to maintain naturalized colonies in appropriate lean, open positions.

planting.note: Direct sow seeds in spring in lean, very well-drained, slightly alkaline to neutral, rocky or sandy soil in full sun; rock pink requires excellent drainage and will rot in heavy, wet soils. It is native to limestone glades, rock outcrops, and dry prairies of the south-central United States. The flowers open in the afternoon and close by evening — a feature of its polemonium-like adaptation to heat-of-the-day bee pollinators.

directions: Plant in lean, very well-drained, rocky or sandy, slightly alkaline to neutral soil in full sun; rock pink is a native succulent perennial of the limestone glades and rocky prairies of the south-central United States — from the Missouri Ozarks south through Kansas and Oklahoma to Texas — producing succulent, cylindrical, bright-green leaves in dense tufts and in summer (June–August) an abundance of bright, clear, rose-pink to magenta-pink, five-petaled flowers (1 inch across) on slender stems that open in the afternoon and close by evening, creating a daily recurrence of cheerful color in hot, dry, full-sun positions. The succulent foliage is attractive even out of flower, providing bright green contrast to the rocky surroundings. It is genuinely adapted to the extreme conditions of limestone glade habitats — thin, rocky, limey soil, baking summer heat, drought, and full sun — conditions that defeat most ornamentals. A rewarding native plant for rock gardens, glade restorations, and any lean, sunny position with well-drained to dry, calcareous soil in zones 4–9. Self-sows modestly to maintain colonies in open, lean-soil conditions.

planting.note: Start seeds indoors 3–4 weeks before last frost or direct sow after soil warms to 70°F in full sun; figleaf gourd requires a long, warm season and performs best in zones 8–11 where the growing season is long enough for fruits to mature. It is a vigorous vine reaching 15–20 feet and requires a strong trellis or fence. Fruits keep for years at room temperature without refrigeration — a unique characteristic shared with no other cucurbit.

directions: Plant in fertile, moist, well-drained soil in full sun on a sturdy trellis; figleaf gourd is one of the most unusual cucurbits in cultivation — an Andean species producing large, distinctive fruits with white-striped, dark-green skin resembling a watermelon but with the elongated form of a large pumpkin, reaching 10–15 pounds. The most remarkable characteristic of this species is the extraordinary keeping quality of its mature fruits: when harvested fully mature, figleaf gourds can keep at room temperature for five or more years without refrigeration, decomposition, or significant quality loss — a storage capability unique among cucurbits and practical in the Andean communities of origin where refrigeration was unavailable. The white, fibrous flesh is used in traditional South American cooking — shredded in sweets, soups, and as a pasta filling — and has an interesting, neutral flavor. The seeds are edible roasted. The vine is large and requires substantial support. More cold-tolerant than many tropical cucurbits and thrives across zones 8–11 where seasons are long. A fascinating and genuinely different crop for the vegetable garden, particularly in Gulf Coast climates.

planting.note: Sow seeds fresh (they lose viability quickly) in well-drained, lean, slightly alkaline to neutral soil in full sun; rock samphire is native to sea cliffs and coastal rocks and requires extremely well-drained, lean, slightly saline soil to thrive. It does not transplant well — direct sow in permanent position. In zones 8–10 it is perennial and quite long-lived in appropriate conditions. Use seeds as soon as possible after harvest — they lose germination viability within a year.

directions: Plant in very well-drained, lean, slightly alkaline to neutral, sandy or rocky soil in full sun; rock samphire is a historical edible coastal herb that was once widely gathered and pickled throughout coastal Europe — Shakespeare referenced its dangerous collection by cliff-hangers in King Lear ('half-way down hangs one that gathers samphire; dreadful trade!') and it was a common market vegetable in London through the 18th century. The finely divided, succulent, gray-green, carrot-family foliage has a strong, distinctive, aromatic flavour combining fennel, anise, and iodine — intensely herbal and marine. It produces flattened umbel flowers in summer followed by seeds. The young shoots are used fresh in salads, cooked as a vegetable, or pickled in vinegar. It grows naturally on sea cliffs, rock crevices, and shingle beaches of the Mediterranean and Atlantic coasts, clinging to completely vertical cliff faces and experiencing salt spray, full sun, and total drought. In the garden it requires similarly lean, well-drained, open conditions. Excellent in rock gardens, coastal gardens, and as a maritime-flavored culinary herb for adventurous cooks.
